subject: ap-government
title: Argument Essay — Constitutional Checks and Balances vs. Social Movements in Representing the People's Will
stimulus: An important feature of a democratic republic is that it represents the will of the people. Take a position on whether constitutional checks and balances or citizen participation in social movements is more effective in ensuring the people's will is represented.

parts.prompt: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning. (Use at least one piece of evidence from one of the following foundational documents: Article I of the United States Constitution, The Federalist 10, "Letter from a Birmingham Jail".)
parts.scoring_notes: Must take a clear position. Generic statements about checks and balances being a feature of the Constitution do not earn the point.

scoring_points.description: Row A — Thesis/Claim: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning.

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: “Checks and balances are more effective in ensuring the people’s will is represented because they guarantee that parts of the government that defy the people will be counteracted or removed entirely.”

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: “Social movements are more effective because they act independently of the government and can force the government to respond to the will of the people.”

wrong_examples: “History has shown that citizen participation in social movements ensures that the people’s will is represented.”
“Checks and balances are a feature of the Constitution.”
common_traps: Restating the prompt without making a claim
Describing the issue without taking a position

parts.prompt: Support your claim with at least TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence. One piece of evidence must come from one of the foundational documents listed. A second piece of evidence can come from any other foundational document or from course concepts.
parts.scoring_notes: To earn 2 or 3 points, must have earned Row A. To earn 3 points, one piece of evidence must come from Article I, Federalist 10, or Letter from Birmingham Jail.

scoring_points.description: Row B — Evidence (0 points): No accurate evidence or evidence not relevant to the topic.

required_elements: No accurate or relevant evidence
alternatives.correct_example: No points earned — evidence is inaccurate or irrelevant to the prompt.

scoring_points.description: Row B — Evidence (1 point): Provides one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt.

required_elements: Provide one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt.
alternatives.correct_example: Provide one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt.

required_elements: May or may not have a claim or thesis.
alternatives.correct_example: May or may not have a claim or thesis.

common_traps: Evidence must be relevant but need not directly support a specific claim to earn 1 point

scoring_points.description: Row B — Evidence (2 points): Uses one piece of specific and relevant evidence to support the claim/thesis OR provides two pieces of relevant evidence.

required_elements: Provide one piece of specific and relevant evidence that supports the claim or thesis. This evidence can come from one of the foundational documents listed in the prompt, any other foundational document, or from knowledge of course concepts.
alternatives.correct_example: Provide one piece of specific and relevant evidence that supports the claim or thesis. This evidence can come from one of the foundational documents listed in the prompt, any other foundational document, or from knowledge of course concepts.

common_traps: To earn 2 points, the response must have earned Row A (claim/thesis)

scoring_points.description: Row B — Evidence (3 points): Uses two pieces of specific and relevant evidence to support the claim, one from a listed foundational document.

required_elements: Provide two pieces of specific and relevant evidence that support the claim or thesis. One of these pieces of evidence must come from a foundational document listed in the prompt. The other piece of evidence can come from a different foundational document or from knowledge of course concepts. Examples that do not earn points: Provide evidence that is not specific
alternatives.correct_example: Provide two pieces of specific and relevant evidence that support the claim or thesis. One of these pieces of evidence must come from a foundational document listed in the prompt. The other piece of evidence can come from a different foundational document or from knowledge of course concepts. Examples that do not earn points: Provide evidence that is not specific

required_elements: “Checks and balances constrain the government.”
alternatives.correct_example: “Checks and balances constrain the government.”

required_elements: “Citizens work in social movements to promote change.” Examples of evidence that are relevant to the topic of the prompt:
alternatives.correct_example: “Citizens work in social movements to promote change.” Examples of evidence that are relevant to the topic of the prompt:

required_elements: Social movements such LGBTQ+, workers, or women’s rights.
alternatives.correct_example: Social movements such LGBTQ+, workers, or women’s rights.

required_elements: Description of any check in the political system.
alternatives.correct_example: Description of any check in the political system.

required_elements: “The women’s suffrage movement used protests and rallies to raise public awareness to promote the right to vote.”
alternatives.correct_example: “The women’s suffrage movement used protests and rallies to raise public awareness to promote the right to vote.”

required_elements: “An elected official that abuses the power of the office is not responsive to the will of the people and can be removed from office through impeachment.”
alternatives.correct_example: “An elected official that abuses the power of the office is not responsive to the will of the people and can be removed from office through impeachment.”

required_elements: “Federalist 10 advocates for a large republic to counter the danger of factions.”
alternatives.correct_example: “Federalist 10 advocates for a large republic to counter the danger of factions.”

required_elements: “Article I establishes checks and balances by requiring both chambers of Congress to pass legislation before being signed or vetoed by the president.”
alternatives.correct_example: “Article I establishes checks and balances by requiring both chambers of Congress to pass legislation before being signed or vetoed by the president.”

required_elements: “‘Letter from a Birmingham Jail’ argues that civil disobedience, which can be used by social movements, is the most effective means for showing the injustices of the system.”
alternatives.correct_example: “‘Letter from a Birmingham Jail’ argues that civil disobedience, which can be used by social movements, is the most effective means for showing the injustices of the system.”

common_traps: One piece must be from a foundational document listed in the prompt

parts.prompt: Use reasoning to explain why your evidence supports your claim or thesis.
parts.scoring_notes: Must have earned Row A and at least 2 points in Row B. Must explain the logical connection between evidence and the thesis.

scoring_points.description: Row C — Reasoning: Use reasoning (classification, process, causation, or comparison) to explain how or why the evidence supports the claim or thesis.

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: “‘In Letter from a Birmingham Jail,’ King explains that waiting for the government to check itself has led to greater suffering for African Americans therefore, social movements increase pressure on policymakers to gain outcomes favorable to their cause.”

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: “Article I provides Congress with the power to override a presidential veto. This power can be very effective in representing the will of the people, because it can be used to stop a president that vetoes legislation for selfish or personal reasons.”

common_traps: Must have earned Row A and at least 2 points in Row B
Simply restating evidence without reasoning does not earn this point

parts.prompt: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal.
parts.scoring_notes: Must have earned Row A. Must describe an alternate perspective AND refute, concede, or rebut it.

scoring_points.description: Row D — Responds to Alternate Perspectives: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using rebuttal or refutation.

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts/refutes it
alternatives.correct_example: “While social movements are broadly based and can pressure the government to respond to what the people want, this is not the same as holding elected officials accountable to the people. Thus, social movements are more limited in what they can achieve, while checks and balances are able to protect against a much larger set of problems.”

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts/refutes it
alternatives.correct_example: “While checks and balances are designed to hold the government accountable to the will of the people, they can also be used to ignore the will of the people. Because social movements are motivated by the people’s desire to hold the government accountable, they are more effective.”

wrong_examples: “Many would argue that social movements are better, but this is simply untrue.”
“Some would argue that social movements are better because they can put extra pressure on government to represent the will of the people.”
common_traps: Simply identifying an alternate perspective without rebutting/refuting it does not earn this point
